content.problemStatement:
You are given a string SSS. A cyclic shift of SSS is a string formed by moving a number of characters from the beginning of SSS to its end (in the same order). Formally, for an integer kkk (0≤k<N0 \le k \lt N0≤k<N), the kkk-th cyclic shift is a string RRR with length NNN such that:


Ri=Si+kR_i = S_{i+k}Ri​=Si+k​ for each 1≤i≤N−k1 \le i \le N-k1≤i≤N−k
Ri=Si−N+kR_i = S_{i-N+k}Ri​=Si−N+k​ for each N−k+1≤i≤NN-k+1 \le i \le NN−k+1≤i≤N
A string is palindromic if it is equal to its own reverse string.

Compute the number of palindromic cyclic shifts of the given string.
constraints: The sum of |S| in all test cases does not exceed 2*10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line of the input contains a single integer T denoting the number of test cases. The description of T test cases follows. The first and only line of each test case contains a single string S.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing one integer — the number of palindromic shifts.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The two palindromic cyclic shifts of 'aabb' are 'abba' and 'baab'.
There are no palindromic cyclic shifts of 'abc'.
The three palindromic cyclic shifts of 'aaa' are 'aaa', 'aaa', and 'aaa'.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can generate all cyclic shifts of the given string and check if each shift is a palindrome. A string is a palindrome if it is equal to its own reverse. We can use a two-pointer approach to check for palindromes.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T*N^2) — where T is the number of test cases and N is the maximum length of the string. This is because for each test case, we generate all cyclic shifts (O(N)) and check if each shift is a palindrome (O(N)).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) — where N is the maximum length of the string. This is because we need to store the string and its reverse.
